blocks.content: The house was a two-bedroom on a quiet street with a yard and a porch and a neighbor named Gerald who always waved.

They moved in three months after the honeymoon. The apartment had been Ethan's first, and there was a negotiation — quiet, good-natured, occasionally heated — about what came with them and what didn't. His sketchbooks: all of them. Her collection of mismatched mugs: most of them. A lamp she loved that he found ugly: it came, and he was wrong about it, and he admitted this eventually.

The small bedroom was for now a studio — Ethan's canvases leaning against one wall, her desk against another. In practice it became the room where things were put when they didn't have another place yet, and that was fine too. They were learning what kind of people they were together.

It turned out they were the kind who cooked together — she prepped, he stirred — and disagreed about the thermostat, and went to bed at different times, and left notes for each other on the counter in the mornings. His were drawn. Hers were written.
It turned out they were the kind who argued rarely but specifically, who recovered quickly, who had figured out somewhere along the way that being right was less important than being okay.
It turned out they were very good at being married.
Not because it was easy. Because they had both decided, separately and together, that it was worth the effort of being good at it.
